data.title: India's market regulator warns Nestle over breach of insider trading regulations
data.description:
Nestle India said on Friday it received a warning from the country's markets regulator for a breach of insider trading regulations "by a designated person of the company."

The Indian arm of Swiss food giant Nestle said its compliance officer received an administrative warning letter from the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) on Thursday. But the firm stopped short of revealing the findings of the letter or the details of the person.

The company, which makes Maggi instant noodles, said there was no material impact on its financials, operations or other activities because of the incident.

Nestle India did not immediately respond to a Reuters' request for comment.

data.title: German bonds regain footing after biggest two-day drop since 1970s
data.description:
A sharp sell-off in euro zone government bonds abated on Friday, after the biggest two-day fall in Bunds since the 1970s on the back of Germany's plans to completely rewrite its fiscal rules.

Investors were waiting for key U.S. jobs figures and were digesting yet more weak factory data from Germany, where industrial orders fell more than expected in January.

Germany's 10-year bond yield (DE10YT=RR), the benchmark for the euro zone bloc, fell 5 basis points (bps) to 2.834%.

"A memorable week is drawing to a close, allowing markets to take stock of the seminal changes in German fiscal politics as well as tariffs and the European Central Bank," said Michael Leister, head of interest rates strategy at Commerzbank.

The yield soared 30 bps on Wednesday, when Germany's plan to change its fiscal rules was announced, the biggest one-day rise since the late 1990s.

It then rose another 10 bps on Thursday after the ECB cut rates but said it was watching European spending plans closely, making the two-day sell-off the biggest since 1974. Yields move inversely to prices.

Pooja Kumra, senior European rates strategist at TD Securities, said she expects Bund yields to fall slightly from here and trade in the 2.4%-2.7% range.

"We still believe that it's hard for Bunds to break their correlation with (U.S.) Treasuries," she said in a research note.

"The negative impulse from tariffs will have a more immediate impact than from the longer-term overhaul of the German economy."

U.S. Treasury yields fell to their lowest level since October at the start of this week after weak manufacturing survey data suggested companies are struggling with unclear tariff policies.

Data later on Friday is expected to show U.S. employment growth picked up in February, but uncertainty over trade could dent the labour market in the months ahead.

Italy's 10-year yield was down 5 bps at 3.894%, leaving the closely watched gap between Italian and German yields (DE10IT10=RR) at 106 bps.

The bonds of more indebted countries such as Italy have sold off alongside those of Germany, which is traditionally the yardstick for the rest of the euro zone, raising government borrowing costs across the bloc.

Germany's two-year bond yield (DE2YT=RR), which is sensitive to ECB rate expectations, was 4 bps lower at 2.24%, but remained up 26 bps for the week.

Earnings Flash (MDA.TO) MDA Space Posts Q4 Revenue C$346.6 Million, vs. FactSet Est of C$319.9M
BP, Jera Report Progress on JV With New Management Team
data.description:
BP (BP.L) and Japanese energy company Jera on Friday are making progress on their plan to start operations of JERA Nex bp by the end of the third quarter.

The companies on Friday unveiled the top management team for the partnership, which will be led by Jera Nex Chief Executive Nathalie Oosterlinck.

The joint venture was announced December 2024 and will have an operating and development assets portfolio of 13 gigawatts of potential net generating capacity across Europe, Asia Pacific and the US.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0555 ET - The Canadian dollar has limited scope to rise after President Trump temporarily delayed tariffs on many goods from Canada and Mexico, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. "Markets had never priced in 25% tariffs as a long-lasting measure, and USD/CAD already corrected from the highs before yesterday's move." Canada also still faces a hit from reciprocal U.S. tariffs in April, he says. USD/CAD could rise to 1.44 in coming weeks, although a potentially softer U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1330 GMT could add some pressure on the exchange rate in the near term, he says. USD/CAD rises 0.1% to 1.4308 after hitting a one-and-a-half-week low of 1.4239 Thursday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0415 ET - Bitcoin falls, even after President Trump signed an executive order Thursday to create a strategic bitcoin reserve and an additional stockpile of other digital assets. Cryptocurrencies are lower as the bitcoin reserve and stockpile will be capitalized by forfeited assets already owned by the government rather than by any new funding, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. The news came ahead of Friday's White House crypto summit. Bitcoin falls 1.3% to $88,237, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Oil prices rise in early trade but remain on track for a significant weekly loss amid fluctuating U.S. trade policies and OPEC+ output hike plans. Brent crude and WTI are both up 1.2% to $70.29 and $67.21 a barrel, respectively, after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the U.S. will intensify pressure on Iran and won't hesitate to go "all in" on sanctions on Russia if it helps in peace negotiations. Still, "oil markets are dealing with renewed uncertainty on the supply side and a darkening outlook on the demand side," analysts at Fitch's research unit BMI say. The U.S. partially pulled back tariffs on some goods from Mexico and Canada, adding further uncertainty over President Trump's trade strategy and its impact on global economic growth. Meanwhile, news that additional OPEC+ supply will soon hit the market is increasing downward pressure on prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1714 ET - The Trump administration says the one-month delay in implementing the 25% tariff on imports from Canada and Mexico will apply to goods considered compliant with USMCA, or the trade treaty. The talk of "USMCA compliant" triggers some head scratching among Canadian business leaders. "Most goods are covered by the trade treaty and Canadian business leaders are encouraged that the Trump Administration recognizes the importance of our trilateral trading pact," says Goldy Hyder, president of the Business Council of Canada, which represents blue-chip CEOs. "Any trade disputes should be worked out under the rules of the USMCA--that's why we have an agreement." (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1629 ET - Canada's largest business group, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, says the Trump administration's latest reprieve on 25% tariffs, until April 2, should be no cause for celebration. "The economy is not a toy to play with. Constant threats and economic uncertainty have taken their toll," says Matthew Holmes, the chamber's executive vice president. Holmes says one way to end the heightened policy uncertainty is to move toward another renegotiation of USMCA, the North American trade treaty, that all countries will respect. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1355 ET - Uncertainty around the federal cost-cutting actions of DOGE is likely to cause private-sector employers to freeze hiring, adding to U.S. labor worries, Evercore ISI analysts write. They estimate that DOGE will directly eliminate a total of 200,000 jobs this year. Canceled government contracts and grants will subtract another nearly 250,000. The resulting paralysis among government contractors would eventually take the final tally to 500,000. The scenario assumes that uncertainties would last for a few months, "resolving in June with DOGE moving on to more structural review." Evercore ISI sees rising odds of a Fed cut in June stemming from DOGE actions. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1343 ET - Lawn-mower maker Toro has significantly reduced its exposure to China over the past few years, with the country currently accounting for a low-single-digit percent of its total cost of goods sold, CEO Rick Olson says on a call with analysts. Some of the company's residential and irrigation products are produced in Mexico, and it doesn't produce in Canada, he adds. Determining a gross impact from tariffs is a moving target right now, Olson says, though he adds that the company is "prepared to quickly make adjustments to our operations and pricing as appropriate." Toro backs its fiscal 2025 outlook, which includes the first round of Chinese tariffs and calls for sales to be flat to up 1% from last year. Toro falls 6.6% after logging lower profit in its F1Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1234 ET - Longer-term Treasury yields recover some ground as the Trump administration dials down the tariffs rhetoric, at lest temporarily. Trump posts on Truth Social that Mexican exports that fall under the USMCA agreement he negotiated in his first term won't be subject to tariffs until April 2. Commerce Secretary Lutnick suggested on CNBC that Canada could also get a reprieve. U.S. trade policy has become a major source of uncertainty roiling financial markets and boosting bets on interest rate cuts by the Fed. Treasury yields up to three years in maturity are falling, with the two-year at 3.985%. Longer-term ones rise, with the 10-year at 4.302%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1155 ET - Agricultural futures are getting a boost from Truth Social posts by President Trump, the most recent one announcing that Mexico won't be required to pay tariffs on any goods that fall under the USMCA agreement through April 2. In his post, Trump thanks President of Mexico Claudia Sheinbaum for negotiating on the topics of curbing illegal migration and the flow of fentanyl over borders. In separate posts, Trump accuses Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau of using tariffs as an issue for his re-election campaign, and in another post blames former President Joe Biden for the record-high U.S. trade deficit seen in January. Agricultural commodities, particularly corn and hogs, saw the biggest boost from the posts. CBOT corn is up 2.5%, while CME lean hogs are up 2.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1141 ET - Growing concerns over tariffs are already affecting pricing decisions and will help to keep inflation sticky, Wells Fargo economists write. They forecast February CPI, due next week, to rise 0.25%, with the core reading advancing 0.27%. The 12-month reading would be 2.9% and 3.2%, respectively, just a little lower than in January. "While February's CPI report is likely to deliver an initial taste of tariffs, it is likely to be just the start," the economists say. They expect CPI to remain above the Fed's 2%. The forecast price increases to "start moving back up this spring and remain stuck near 3% for the duration of this year." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1050 ET - Livestock futures trading on the CME are reacting to comments made on CNBC by Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, saying that cutouts from the tariffs on Mexico and Canada earlier this week will "not likely" be limited to just automakers, with President Trump expected to act to include goods "compliant" with USMCA rules as the sides negotiate a longer-term deal. Mexico is the largest market for U.S. pork, so analysts and traders are hopeful pork will be covered. Most-active lean hog futures are up 2.2%, after Lutnick's comments, while live cattle sinks 0.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1039 ET - Initial indications of a softening U.S. labor market are likely to increase in coming months as the Trump administration cuts federal jobs en masse, Evercore ISI analysts write. They estimate that DOGE will, directly or indirectly, shave off nearly 500,000 payrolls this year, with the bulk of it starting to show in March labor figures. Evercore ISI expects federal layoffs to impact the private sector, as contracts are rolled back and concerned employers freeze hiring. The analysts say the impact could be even larger. The layoffs could increase the chances of an interest rate cut by the Fed in June, Evercore ISI says, adding that the disinflationay effect could be offset my immigration restrictions. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.description:
The BSE SENSEX Index is up 1134.48 points or 1.55% this week to 74332.58

  • Snaps a three-week losing streak

data.title: EU Natural Gas Rebounds from 5-Month Low
data.description:
European natural gas futures rebounded to €40/MWh on Friday from the five-month low of €36.5/MWh earlier in the session after Russian forces struck Ukrainian gas infrastructure with drones and missiles, threatening supply to EU members that still receive gas from the transit area.

The attack trimmed the sharp losses from the week, stemmed by a series summits between EU members raised optimism that the union would step up efforts to seek alternative gas sources outside of Russia.

Gas prices were also pressured by seeming concessions that Brussels may give for Slovakia regarding the source of its gas supply after the Slovak delegation conceded in backing plans for EU defense spending and Ukraine aid.

Additionally, gas prices were also lower this week amid warmer weather through the continent, easing demand at the end of the winter, and the possibility that the EU would be less strict about storage targets in coming years.

data.title: MDA Space Sees 2025 Rev C$1.50B-C$1.65B >MDA.T
Baidu Eyes $2 Billion Bond Offering
data.description:
Baidu has proposed an international offering of exchangeable bonds in principal amount of $2 billion due 2032 to repay its certain existing indebtedness and interest accrued and to replenish its funds for general corporate purposes, a Friday Hong Kong bourse filing said.

The bonds will reference ordinary shares of Trip.com Group but not its American depository shares.

The bonds will be offered outside the U.S. in offshore transactions to non-U.S. persons.

The exchange ratio and other terms of the bonds are yet to be finalized.

data.title: Pound heads for worst weekly run versus euro since 2023
data.description:
Key points:
  • European fiscal changes in focus
  • Sterling overshadowed by economic forecasts due on March 26
  • Investor sentiment shaped by transformed outlook for European defence

The pound headed for its worst weekly performance against the euro in over two years on Friday, as a boost to European spending drove a broad rally in the single currency, while against the dollar, sterling rose ahead of U.S. jobs data.

The euro has surged across the board this week, logging its best weekly performance against the dollar since March 2009.

Against the pound, it was set for a weekly gain of 1.5% , the most since January 2023.

It was last up 0.4% at 84.03 pence. The pound was up 0.4% against the dollar at $1.292.

European leaders have rallied round Ukraine, with a view to reaching a peace deal, and pledged to spend more on their own defence in view of the shift in the U.S. stance under President Donald Trump.

Spending of around $1 trillion, spurred by Germany's overhaul of its fiscal rules to release funds for infrastructure and defence, as well as European Union efforts to increase its joint borrowing, have transformed investor sentiment.

Equity investors have poured money in at the fastest rate in almost a decade and the euro has soared in response, bringing gains versus the pound to 1.2% so far this year and to 1.1% against the dollar. Sterling by contrast is up just 0.4% against the U.S. currency.

The pound has drawn support from the expectation among traders that the Bank of England will have to cut rates more slowly than other central banks, including the Federal Reserve.

Yet the focus for investors has shifted away from the interest-rate debate and towards the outlook for global capital flows. In particular, they are seeking alternatives to the United States, given a darkening economic outlook and high uncertainty over trade and fiscal policy.

The mood around the pound is nervous.

On March 26, the Office for Budget Responsibility, the UK public finances watchdog, issues its own forecasts on the economy and borrowing.

Sluggish economic growth and higher government borrowing costs have put finance minister Rachel Reeves under pressure to clarify how she intends to balance the books without breaking her own fiscal rules.

"A light data calendar has seen the pound continue to trade in the shadow of the euro," analysts at Monex Europe said.

"It is also not a constructive environment for sterling in our view, with growing noises around the difficult decisions facing UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves. We suspect this will become a major focus in the coming weeks, ahead of the March 26 economic update," they said.

data.title: Mistral AI Is Open to Partnering With U.S. Telecom Companies, CEO Says — Market Talk
data.description: French artificial-intelligence startup Mistral AI is open to teaming up with telecommunication companies in the U.S., Chief Executive Officer Arthur Mensch said in an interview at the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona. "That would be on the table," he said, noting that some discussions were taking place. Mistral AI is already partnering with telecommunication companies in Europe, including Orange and Free, integrating Mistral AI's applications into some of their products and using AI to improve network performance. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: MDA Space Sees 2025 Adjusted Ebitda C$290M-C$320M >MDA.T
Market Chatter: iFlyTek Looks to Grow Europe Operations Amid China-US Trade Spat
data.description:
iFlyTek is aiming to expand its operations in Europe amid the trade war with the US and China, Reuters reported Thursday, citing an interview with Vice President Vincent Zhan.

Trade tensions between China and the US affect the artificial intelligence firm as North America is their largest market outside of China in terms of demand, the report said, citing Zhan.

iFlyTek is looking to diversify its supply chain to mitigate the impact from the trade war, the report said.

Zhan's remarks on the sidelines of the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona come after US President Donald Trump slapped 20% tariffs on Chinese electronics categories that have not been touched by prior levies, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Panoro Energy Outlines Oil Discovery Offshore Gabon
data.description:
Panoro Energy announced a significant new oil discovery at the Bourdon prospect offshore Gabon.

The Norway-listed exploration and development company holds a 17.5% participating interest in the prospect, according to a Friday filing.
